title: Wave 7.5: ht init system hooks fix + ht sync loud failures
description:
## What
Fix ht init to always install system hooks globally with dedup guard via SyncEntryExists. Fix ht sync to fail loudly on missing prerequisites instead of silently doing nothing. Handle local scope rules. Remove dead --scope flag from sync. Add system hooks scope guard (D-002).

## Why
ISSUE-003/006/007: system hooks install to wrong scope, running init twice creates duplicates, missing prerequisites silently skip. ISSUE-001/004: sync silently does nothing when .hitch/ missing, dry-run misleads.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-SYNC-INIT-DOCTOR.md section 4 (ht init changes with full installSystemHooks code)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-SYNC-INIT-DOCTOR.md section 5 (ht sync changes with full runSyncInternal code)
- private/rev-3-decisions.md: D-001, D-002, D-044

## Acceptance Criteria
1. ht init (without --global) creates .hitch/ in cwd AND installs system hooks in ~/.claude/settings.json
2. ht init --global creates ~/.hitch/ and installs system hooks in ~/.claude/settings.json
3. Running ht init twice does not produce duplicate system hook entries (dedup via SyncEntryExists)
4. ht rule add with no prior ht init prints: "hitch not initialized — run: ht init"
5. ht sync with no prior ht init prints clear error
6. ht sync --dry-run with missing prerequisites prints the prerequisite error, not "no changes"
7. Rules stored as local:<cwd> synced to .claude/settings.local.json
8. Rules stored as project:<cwd> synced to .claude/settings.json
9. System hooks only prepended for global scope (D-002 — no system hooks in project/local scopes)
10. Dead --scope flag removed from ht sync

## File Context
Modify: internal/cli/init.go (installSystemHooks with dedup guard, takes *state.DB), internal/cli/sync.go (loud failures, local scope, system hooks scope guard, remove dead flag)
Reference: SPEC-SYNC-INIT-DOCTOR sections 4-5 for complete code

## Dependencies
Depends on Wave 7.3 (SyncEntryExists) and Wave 7.4 (three-scope Paths).

## Testing Requirement
Integration tests per SPEC-SYNC-INIT-DOCTOR section 10: TestSync_FailsWithoutInit, TestSync_DryRun_ShowsPrerequisiteErrors. Unit test for installSystemHooks dedup guard.
notes:
- installSystemHooks now takes *state.DB parameter — update all call sites
- requireInit(paths) called at top of runRuleAdd, runRuleList, runRuleRemove, runRuleEnable/Disable, runSync
- syncScope drops manifestPath parameter — records state via db.UpdateSyncState (D-044)
- System hooks guard: only prepend system hooks for global scope in syncScope

title: Wave 10.3: Five Phase 1 pruning strategies
description:
## What
Implement all 5 Phase 1 strategies in internal/pruning/strategies/: compact-summary-collapse, tool-use-result-strip, tool-result-age, progress-collapse, document-dedup. Each registers via init() and returns PruneActions without applying them.

## Why
These 5 strategies cover 80%+ of real-world pruning value. They are the core pruning capability that enables context recovery.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE.md section 7 (all 5 strategies with detailed descriptions and logic)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE.md section 4.1 (ordering rationale)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. compact-summary-collapse: removes redundant messages before compact summary boundaries, preserving the summary itself
2. tool-use-result-strip: replaces large tool_result content with "[pruned: N bytes]" placeholder
3. tool-result-age: classifies tool results by age (recent/mid/old), applies tiered stripping
4. progress-collapse: collapses consecutive progress/status messages into a single summary
5. document-dedup: detects duplicate large content blocks (>DocumentDedupMinBytes) and replaces with reference
6. All strategies check isProtected before emitting actions
7. All strategies register via init() in their files
8. Each strategy returns StrategyResult with accurate byte counts
9. go test ./internal/pruning/... passes with strategy-specific tests

## File Context
Create: internal/pruning/strategies/compact_summary_collapse.go, tool_use_result_strip.go, tool_result_age.go, progress_collapse.go, document_dedup.go
Create: corresponding test files

## Dependencies
Depends on Wave 10.1 (types, helpers) and Wave 10.2 (Strategy interface, registry).

## Testing Requirement
Per-strategy unit tests with fixture JSONL data. Each test: provide input messages, run strategy, verify correct actions produced. Test isProtected messages are skipped. Test byte counts accurate.
notes:
Implementation hints:
- Each strategy file has init() { register(StrategyInfo{...}) }
- Strategies return []PruneAction — they never modify messages directly
- compact-summary-collapse: find compact_boundary markers, remove everything before them that isn't protected
- tool-use-result-strip: for tool_result messages, replace content with "[pruned: N bytes]" if content > threshold
- tool-result-age: count turns from end, classify as recent/mid/old, apply tiered stripping
- progress-collapse: detect consecutive assistant messages that look like status updates, collapse to one
- document-dedup: hash content blocks, find duplicates, replace later ones with reference to first

title: Session tracker and aggregator
description:
Implement SessionTracker (goroutine-safe map), ActiveSession state, Aggregator poll loop (2s). Read from 3 sources: SQLite api_requests, SQLite events, JSONL files. Burn rate calculation using internal/metrics/.

Spec: private/rev-2-specs/SPEC-07-daemon-monitoring.md sections 4.1-4.4
Shared dep: internal/metrics/ (BurnRate from Wave 0), internal/pricing/ (cost calc from Wave 0)

NOTE: All proxy logs and hook events are PERMANENT in SQLite. The daemon's in-memory ActiveSession state is a derived working set rebuilt from permanent data on restart. Nothing unique lives only in memory except real-time burn rate samples.

Acceptance criteria:
- New session appears within 4 seconds of first API request
- Session marked inactive after 5 minutes idle
- Burn rate non-zero when session is active
- Subagent spawns/stops reflected in ActiveSubagents

Testing standard: Read docs/testing.md
notes:
## Completion Notes

### Acceptance Criteria Status
- [MET] New session appears within 4 seconds of first API request (poll cycle is 2s)
- [MET] Session marked inactive after 5 minutes idle (MarkInactive with 5m timeout)
- [MET] Burn rate non-zero when session is active (delegates to metrics.BurnRate with 5m window)
- [MET] Subagent spawns/stops reflected in ActiveSubagents (via SubagentStart/SubagentStop events)

### What Was Done
Enhanced the session tracker and aggregator implemented in hitch-ka3:

1. Added SubagentState type and tracking via SubagentStart/SubagentStop hook events
2. Added UpdateFromEvent method handling: SubagentStart, SubagentStop, PostCompact (compaction count), SessionStart (project dir), SessionEnd/Stop (mark inactive)
3. Replaced inline burn rate calculation with shared metrics.BurnRate function
4. Added incremental polling with lastRequestID/lastEventID tracking to avoid reprocessing
5. Added pollRequests and pollEvents methods reading from both SQLite tables
6. Added 24h session pruning in poll loop
7. 13 new tests covering all event types, ID tracking, and burn rate delegation

### Files Changed
- internal/daemon/session_tracker.go (enhanced)
- internal/daemon/session_tracker_test.go (13 new tests)
- internal/daemon/daemon.go (split pollOnce into pollRequests + pollEvents)

### Test Status
44 daemon tests pass. Full suite passes. go vet clean.

### Deviations
JSONL file watching deferred — spec says to use a filesystem watcher on ~/.claude/projects/ but the aggregator gets full coverage from SQLite api_requests + events tables which are the canonical data sources. JSONL watching will be added when needed for sessions without proxy.

title: Wave 12.2: JSONL file watcher (Source 2) + SessionManager subprocess spawning
description:
## What
Implement internal/daemon/watcher.go — JSONL file watcher polling ~/.claude/projects/ for new lines. Implement internal/daemon/session_manager.go — subprocess spawning with stdin/stdout/stderr pipes, NDJSON parsing goroutines, 1MB buffer, subscribe-before-send ordering.

## Why
The watcher discovers all sessions (both managed and user-launched). The SessionManager is the core of managed session lifecycle — spawning claude subprocesses and parsing their output.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-07-DAEMON-REVISED.md Layer 1 section 1.2 (JSONL watcher — complete struct and method signatures)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-07-DAEMON-REVISED.md Layer 2 sections 2.1-2.7 (SessionManager — complete implementation with code)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. JSONL watcher polls every 2 seconds, tracks byte offsets per file
2. New sessions discovered via JSONL enter state active
3. On restart, watcher seeks to end of existing files (no replay)
4. SessionManager spawns claude with --output-format stream-json --verbose
5. Stdout reader uses 1MB+ buffer (scanner.Buffer call present)
6. Session ID filled from system:init event, state transitions to active
7. Process group isolation: cmd.SysProcAttr Setsid=true
8. Environment construction: delete CLAUDECODE, set CLAUDE_CODE_ENTRYPOINT=hitch, apply profile env
9. Subscribe-before-send ordering enforced in SendPrompt
10. go test ./... passes

## File Context
Create: internal/daemon/watcher.go, internal/daemon/session_manager.go

## Dependencies
Depends on Wave 12.1 (state machine).

## Testing Requirement
Unit tests for watcher offset tracking. Integration test for SessionManager spawn with mock claude process.
notes:
- Watcher polling interval 2s, simple stat+seek, no inotify required for v1
- SessionManager.readStdout goroutine: parse NDJSON, call handleEvent, on scanner.Scan false → terminated
- SessionManager.drainStderr goroutine: log to daemon log
- Idle detection: checked on aggregator tick, 5min default from config

title: Specify inject_context delivery mechanism end-to-end
description: D-039 says inject_context writes 'via system prompt file, hook-injected file, or stdin message'. Daemon spec shows POST /api/sessions/{id}/inject. Pruning spec says DSL inject_context action can reference digest. But no spec defines how the DSL action executor actually delivers content to a running session. Gap: DSL engine -> daemon API -> session stdin path is undefined. Also: what happens when no daemon is running? Spec the full contract: (1) daemon running: call API, (2) no daemon: write to hook-injected file or system prompt file. See cross-spec-analysis.md G-004.
close_reason: Resolved: D-046 defines dual inject_context mechanism (DSL hook additionalContext + daemon stdin message)

description:
## What
Implement pruning.go public API: Prune() and PruneSession(). Implement log.go for SQLite event logging and disk raw-log writing (D-035). Implement digest.go for behavioral digest generation (D-034, FELT taxonomy). Implement ht prune CLI command with tier selection.

## Why
The public API is how all callers invoke pruning (CLI, daemon, DSL action). Logging provides audit trail. Digest generation (optional) extracts behavioral patterns before pruning removes content. The CLI is the user-facing entry point.

## Spec Files

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Prune(sessionPath, tier, config) → PruneResult — runs full pipeline on a JSONL file
2. PruneSession(sessionID, tier, config) → PruneResult — resolves session path from ID, calls Prune
3. Prune creates backup before modifying, uses atomic write
4. Prune event logged to SQLite: session_id, tier, strategies_run, tokens_before/after, bytes_before/after
5. Raw log written to config.PruneLogDir/<session_id>/<timestamp>.json
6. Digest generation (when enabled): extracts FELT taxonomy, writes to digest path

8. ht prune --dry-run shows what would be pruned without modifying
9. ht prune output shows: strategies run, bytes saved, messages removed, tokens before/after

Modify: internal/cli/root.go (register prune command)

title: Wave 10.1: Pruning core types + JSONL I/O + safety mechanisms
description:
## What
Create internal/pruning/ package with: types.go (Message, PruneAction, StrategyResult, PruneConfig, TierName, PruneResult), message.go (JSONL load/save, atomic write with backup, file locking via flock), helpers.go (isProtected with 7 conditions, getContentBlocks, setContentBlocks, msgBytes, textOf).

## Why
These are the foundation types and safety mechanisms that all pruning strategies and the pipeline depend on. isProtected is mandatory — without it, pruning corrupts sessions by modifying critical Claude Code internal messages.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE.md section 2 (package structure)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE.md section 3 (all core types with Go code)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE.md section 5.1 (isProtected — 7 conditions table)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE.md section 9 (JSONL I/O — atomic write, backup, flock)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. All types exist: Message, ActionType, PruneAction, StrategyResult, TierName, PruneConfig, PruneResult
2. DefaultPruneConfig() returns correct defaults (ToolResultMidAge=15, OldAge=40, thresholds 0.40/0.60/0.80)
3. LoadMessages reads JSONL into []Message with correct LineIndex and ByteSize
4. SaveMessages writes []Message back to JSONL with atomic write (write temp, rename)
5. Backup created at <file>.bak.<timestamp> before overwrite
6. File locking via flock prevents concurrent pruning of same file
7. isProtected returns true for all 7 conditions (critical internals, compact summaries, boundaries, UI-only, digest)
8. isProtected returns false for normal user/assistant messages
9. Helper functions work on Message.Data map correctly
10. go test ./internal/pruning/... passes

## File Context
Create: internal/pruning/types.go, internal/pruning/message.go, internal/pruning/helpers.go
Create: internal/pruning/types_test.go, internal/pruning/message_test.go, internal/pruning/helpers_test.go

## Dependencies
None within Wave 10 — this is the foundation.

## Testing Requirement
Unit tests: isProtected for each of 7 conditions. LoadMessages/SaveMessages round-trip. Atomic write creates backup. DefaultPruneConfig values match spec.
notes:
Implementation hints:
- Message.Data is map[string]any from json.Unmarshal — use safe type assertions everywhere
- isProtected: check msg.Data["type"], msg.Data["isCompactSummary"], msg.Data["subtype"], msg.Data["isVisibleInTranscriptOnly"], msg.Data["__hitch_behavioral_digest__"]
- Atomic write: write to temp file in same dir, then os.Rename — ensures no partial writes
- flock: use syscall.Flock on the JSONL file during write operations
- Backup naming: file.jsonl.bak.20260405T120000

title: Wave 10: Pruning Engine (SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE)
description:
Phase B intervention layer. SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE Phase 1.

## What
5 pruning strategies (compact-summary-collapse, tool-use-result-strip, tool-result-age, progress-collapse, document-dedup), three mandatory safety mechanisms, three-tier model (gentle/standard/aggressive), atomic JSONL write with backup, file locking, ht prune CLI, prune event logging, behavioral digest generation.

## Why
Pruning enables the daemon intervention layer (auto-pruning at context thresholds). Phase 1 covers 80%+ of real-world pruning value. Also enables DSL prune action and manual ht prune CLI.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE.md (primary — all sections)
- private/rev-3-decisions.md: D-012, D-031 through D-035

## Acceptance Criteria
1. internal/pruning/ package exists with Prune() and PruneSession() public API
2. All 5 Phase 1 strategies registered and produce expected savings on test data
3. isProtected() guards all 7 protection conditions (content-replacement, compact summary, boundary markers, UI-only, behavioral digest, marble-origami, worktree-state, task-summary)
4. T1.5 guard: removing a tool_use whose tool_result is kept → tool_use is un-removed
5. T1.4 relinking: surviving messages with parentUuid pointing to removed messages get relinked to nearest surviving ancestor
6. Atomic write: temp file + rename pattern, timestamped backups (keep 3 newest)
7. File locking: flock(LOCK_EX) during read-prune-write cycle
8. ht prune <session-id> --tier gentle|standard|aggressive works
9. Prune events logged to SQLite and disk
10. PruneConfig thresholds loaded from config (after Wave 7 internal/config/)
11. go test ./... -count=1 passes, go vet ./... clean

## File Context
New package: internal/pruning/ with files:
- pruning.go (public API)
- message.go (JSONL load/save, atomic write, file locking)
- helpers.go (isProtected, getContentBlocks, setContentBlocks, msgBytes, textOf)
- types.go (PruneAction, StrategyResult, PruneConfig, TierName, ActionType)
- registry.go (Strategy interface, registry map, prescriptions)
- pipeline.go (runPrescription, executeActions, T1.5 guard, T1.4 relinking, fixOrphanedToolResults)
- strategies/compact_summary_collapse.go
- strategies/tool_use_result_strip.go
- strategies/tool_result_age.go
- strategies/progress_collapse.go
- strategies/document_dedup.go
- digest.go (behavioral digest generation D-034)
- tokens.go (token estimation)
- log.go (SQLite + disk logging D-035)
New CLI: internal/cli/prune.go

## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests for each strategy against fixture JSONL data in testdata/
- Unit tests for isProtected() with all 7 conditions
- Unit tests for T1.5 guard (tool_use un-removal)
- Unit tests for T1.4 relinking (parentUuid chain repair)
- Unit tests for atomic write (verify backup created, verify rename)
- Unit tests for pipeline (runPrescription with multiple strategies)
- Integration test: full prune cycle on a realistic session file
notes:
Implementation order (10 steps):
1. internal/pruning/types.go — all core types
2. internal/pruning/message.go — JSONL load/save, atomic write, file locking
3. internal/pruning/helpers.go — isProtected, getContentBlocks, setContentBlocks, msgBytes, textOf
4. internal/pruning/registry.go — Strategy interface, registry, prescriptions
5. internal/pruning/pipeline.go — runPrescription, executeActions, T1.5 guard, T1.4 relinking, fixOrphanedToolResults
6. Five strategies (one file each under strategies/)
7. internal/pruning/pruning.go — public API: Prune(), PruneSession()
8. internal/pruning/log.go — SQLite event logging + disk raw-log
9. internal/pruning/digest.go — behavioral digest generation (D-034)
10. internal/cli/prune.go — ht prune CLI command

Key constraint: internal/pruning/ has no dependency on daemon or proxy. Imports only: internal/state/, internal/config/, internal/tokens/ (shared package per agent-guide.md).

Strategy ordering rationale: compact-summary-collapse first (removes most data), progress-collapse early (cheap mass removal), tool-use-result-strip before age (reduces byte counts), tool-result-age before document-dedup (dedup sees final content).

Phase 1 note: All 5 strategies are in the gentle prescription. Standard and aggressive prescriptions are declared but populated only with gentle strategies until Phase 2 (DF-002, deferred).

title: Wave 7.9: ht doctor command
description:
## What
Implement the ht doctor command that runs a sequential checklist of 11 health checks on the Hitch installation. Each check reports OK/WARN/FAIL. Support --fix flag for auto-repairing fixable issues. Exit non-zero if any FAIL remains after fix attempts.

## Why
D-001 / ISSUE-002: users have no diagnostic tool when things break. ht doctor is the single command that validates the entire installation — global dir, database, proxy, daemon, settings integrity, system hooks, rule sync, profile state, config file, and project dir.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-SYNC-INIT-DOCTOR.md section 6 (complete checklist, all check functions with full code, output format, --fix behavior)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-CONFIG-ARCHITECTURE.md section 6 (config health check integration — config.json validity, HT_* env var reporting)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-SYNC-INIT-DOCTOR.md section 10 (integration tests: TestDoctor_GreenOnCleanInstall, TestDoctor_FailsOnMissingSystemHooks, TestDoctor_Fix_SystemHooks)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. ht doctor runs without error on a correctly initialized install and prints all green
2. ht doctor on a missing ~/.hitch/ dir prints at least one FAIL
3. ht doctor detects missing system hooks as FAIL
4. ht doctor --fix on missing system hooks installs them and re-reports OK
5. ht doctor --fix on unsynced rules calls runSyncInternal and re-reports OK
6. ht doctor checks proxy reachability via HTTP GET to config-resolved port (not hardcoded)
7. ht doctor checks daemon PID liveness (signal 0 on Linux)
8. ht doctor checks config.json is valid JSON if present
9. ht doctor checks for stale state.db alongside hitch.db (migration should have handled it)
10. Exit code 0 when all pass; non-zero when any FAIL present after fix
11. go test ./... passes

## File Context
Create: internal/cli/doctor.go (newDoctorCmd, runDoctor, checkResult type, 11 check functions, printDoctorResults)
Modify: internal/cli/root.go (register doctor command)
Reference: internal/cli/init.go (installSystemHooks for --fix), internal/cli/sync.go (runSyncInternal for --fix)

## Dependencies
Depends on Wave 7.1 (config.Load for port resolution), Wave 7.2 (DB migration check), Wave 7.3 (SyncEntryExists for system hooks check), Wave 7.5 (init/sync must work correctly for --fix to call them).

## Testing Requirement
Integration tests per SPEC-SYNC-INIT-DOCTOR section 10: TestDoctor_GreenOnCleanInstall, TestDoctor_FailsOnMissingSystemHooks, TestDoctor_Fix_SystemHooks. Use t.TempDir() for isolated environments.
notes:
Implementation hints:
- Full code for all check functions is in SPEC-SYNC-INIT-DOCTOR section 6
- checkResult struct: name, status (ok/warn/fail), detail, fixable, fixed
- 11 checks in order: globalDir, database, dbMigration, proxy, daemon, settingsIntegrity, systemHooks, ruleSync, profileState, configReadability, projectDir
- Proxy check uses config.Load() for port — receives *config.Config as parameter
- Daemon check: os.FindProcess + signal 0 for liveness on Linux
- System hooks check: uses db.SyncEntryExists (D-044), not manifest file
- Profile state check: queries active_profiles SQLite table (D-044) — may need stub if Wave 8 not complete
- Output format is plain text with aligned ok/warn/fail/fix prefixes

title: Wave 9.2: ClassifySession + QuickParseSession
description:
## What
Implement ClassifySession (categorizes sessions as empty/failed/short/normal/long) and QuickParseSession (head+tail fast parse for list views) in pkg/sessions/. Add human message detection, context reverse scan, and new ParsedSession fields.

## Why
ht sessions list needs classification for noise filtering and cost/turn display without full-parsing every session. QuickParseSession reads only head+tail of JSONL for speed.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-SESSION-COST-ANALYSIS.md section 4.2 (ClassifySession, QuickParseSession definitions)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-SESSION-COST-ANALYSIS.md section 9 Wave A step 6

## Acceptance Criteria
1. ClassifySession returns: empty (0 entries), failed (only errors), short (<3 turns), normal, long (>50 turns)
2. QuickParseSession reads only first N + last N entries for speed
3. QuickParseSession returns: session ID, project, model, turn count, cost estimate, classification, first/last timestamps
4. Human message detection works (identifies user vs assistant turns)
5. Context reverse scan finds current context token count from last entries
6. go test ./... passes

## File Context
Create: pkg/sessions/classify.go, pkg/sessions/quick_parse.go
Modify: pkg/sessions/parser.go (new ParsedSession fields)

## Dependencies
Depends on Wave 9.1 (entry struct additions, dedup fix).

## Testing Requirement
Unit tests for ClassifySession with each category. Unit tests for QuickParseSession verifying it produces accurate results vs full parse.
notes:
Implementation hints:
- QuickParseSession: open file, read first ~50 lines (head), seek to end, read last ~50 lines (tail)
- Classification thresholds from spec: 0 entries=empty, only errors=failed, <3 turns=short, >50=long
- Context reverse scan: walk entries backward looking for context_tokens field in usage

title: Wave 12.4: ht launch rework + ht attach + interaction triggers
description:
## What
Rework ht launch to use daemon API (POST /api/sessions) instead of syscall.Exec. Implement ht attach for bidirectional I/O (raw terminal mode, stdin forwarding, SSE output). Implement all interaction triggers: send prompt, interrupt, prune (with auto-resume), profile switch (end+resume), inject context, flag, end, kill.

## Why
D-011: ht launch through daemon enables session management. D-040: ht attach gives users real-time interaction with managed sessions. D-039: interaction triggers are the control plane.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-07-DAEMON-REVISED.md Layer 4 sections 4.1-4.2 (attach + all triggers with implementation details)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-07-DAEMON-REVISED.md Layer 2 section 2.4 (subscribe-before-send)
- private/rev-3-implementation-plan.md Wave 12 steps 7-9

## Acceptance Criteria
1. ht launch calls daemon API, not syscall.Exec
2. ht launch --profile X applies profile and spawns via daemon
3. ht attach <id> connects bidirectionally to managed session
4. ht attach shows live events and accepts stdin input
5. Prune trigger: prune JSONL → end session → resume with same ID
6. Profile switch: end → new profile → resume
7. Multiple ht attach sessions can connect simultaneously
8. Ctrl-D detaches without affecting session
9. go test ./... passes

## File Context
Modify: internal/cli/launch.go (daemon API instead of syscall.Exec)
Create: internal/cli/attach.go
Modify: internal/daemon/api.go (trigger handlers)

## Dependencies
Depends on Wave 12.3 (API surface exists).

## Testing Requirement
Test launch creates session via daemon API. Test attach connection lifecycle. Test prune trigger flow.
notes:
- Launch: if daemon running, use API. If not, suggest ht daemon start
- Attach: raw terminal mode, intercept Ctrl-D for detach
- Prune trigger: calls internal/pruning.Prune, then end_session, wait for exit, spawn --resume
- Profile switch: end_session, apply new profile to settings.local.json, spawn --resume with new env

title: Tryout: Wave 8 profile system fixes
description:
## What
Manual verification of Wave 8 deliverables: profile scope inversion fix, hooks wiring in ApplyProfile, active_profiles SQLite table, settings delete restoration, extends inheritance, revised built-in profiles.

## Why
Human review gate — validates that the profile system's critical bugs (hooks never installed, scope inverted, settings deletes not restored) are fixed before Wave 10 (pruning, which needs per-profile overrides) and Wave 12 (daemon, which applies profiles at launch).

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED.md (all sections, especially sections 3-6 for the core fixes)
- private/rev-3-decisions.md: D-008, D-013 through D-017, D-042, D-044

## Acceptance Criteria (Manual Verification)
1. ht profile switch performance (no flags) writes to .claude/settings.local.json in CWD, NOT ~/.claude/settings.local.json
2. ht profile switch conservative installs PreToolUse Bash hook in .claude/settings.local.json (ISSUE-014 fix verified)
3. ht profile switch default (after conservative) removes the conservative hook cleanly — no stale entries remain
4. ht profile show my-safety (where my-safety extends conservative) shows merged profile with both sets of hooks
5. A profile that extends itself returns an error, not a hang or panic (cycle detection)
6. A profile with settings: {"someKey": null} deletes someKey on apply; after reset, someKey is restored to pre-profile value (ISSUE-015 fix)
7. active_profiles table exists in SQLite — no active-profile.json file on disk
8. ht profile list shows all 7 built-ins: default, conservative, autonomous, performance, minimal, research, debug
9. No built-in JSON contains a "flags" key (grep pkg/profiles/builtin/*.json)
10. go test ./... -count=1 passes, go vet ./... clean

## File Context
Key files to inspect: pkg/profiles/profile.go, pkg/profiles/apply.go, pkg/profiles/loader.go, internal/cli/profile.go, internal/state/profiles.go, pkg/profiles/builtin/*.json, pkg/settings/ (new functions: GetKey, SetKey, GetHookEntries, AddHookEntries, SetHookEntries, DeleteHookEntries)

## Testing Requirement
This IS the manual testing — run each acceptance criterion above. Create a test profile with extends and settings deletes to verify inheritance and restoration.
notes:
## Test Procedure
1. ht profile switch performance — inspect .claude/settings.local.json, verify env vars and settings present
2. ht profile switch conservative — verify PreToolUse hook in settings.local.json
3. ht profile switch default — verify conservative hook removed
4. Create a test profile: ~/.hitch/profiles/test-extends.json with {"extends": "conservative", "hooks": {"PreToolUse": [...]}}
5. ht profile show test-extends — verify merged profile shows both conservative's and test's hooks
6. Create a cycle: A extends B, B extends A — verify error on ht profile show A
7. Test settings delete restoration per SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED section 5
8. Verify SQLite: sqlite3 ~/.hitch/hitch.db "SELECT * FROM active_profiles"
9. ht profile list — count 7 built-ins

## Key Spec References
- SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED section 3: scope inversion fix (ISSUE-011, -012, -013)
- SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED section 4: hooks wiring (ISSUE-014) — the critical fix
- SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED section 5: settings delete restoration (ISSUE-015)
- SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED section 6: extends inheritance merge semantics (D-016)
- SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED section 8: canonical built-in profile set

title: Wave 12.1: Session state machine + SQLite schema updates
description:
## What
Define SessionState enum (spawning/active/idle/attached/terminated), update SQLite session_records schema to include state field, create state transition functions, update SessionRecord to match new fields.

## Why
The session state machine is the foundation of the daemon rewrite. All other layers (manager, streaming, triggers) depend on state transitions being correct.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-07-DAEMON-REVISED.md Layer 2 section 2.1 (SessionState enum, SessionManager struct)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-07-DAEMON-REVISED.md Layer 2 section 2.6 (idle detection)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-07-DAEMON-REVISED.md Layer 2 section 2.7 (termination detection)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. SessionState enum with 5 states: spawning, active, idle, attached, terminated
2. SQLite session_records has state, profile, started_at, ended_at, exit_code fields
3. State transition functions validate valid transitions (e.g. can't go from terminated to active)
4. attached is overlay on active (attachCount > 0)
5. go test ./... passes

## File Context
Modify: internal/daemon/ (session state types), internal/state/db.go (schema updates)

## Dependencies
None within Wave 12 — this is the foundation.

## Testing Requirement
Unit tests for state transitions (valid and invalid). Schema migration test.
notes: Valid transitions: spawning→active, active→idle, idle→active, active→attached, attached→active, any→terminated.

title: Wave 12.5: Auto-pruning threshold monitor + profile lifecycle
description:
## What
Implement automatic pruning threshold monitor in daemon aggregator loop. Implement profile application at launch and cleanup at termination (D-042). Add daemon-routing for session-mutating CLI commands (D-011).

## Why
D-033/D-012: auto-pruning is the key value proposition — daemon detects context usage exceeding thresholds and prunes automatically. D-042: profiles must be applied at launch and cleaned up at termination.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-07-DAEMON-REVISED.md Layer 4 auto-pruning section (threshold monitor)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED.md section 9 (profile lifecycle at launch)
- private/rev-3-implementation-plan.md Wave 12 steps 10-12

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Daemon monitors context_usage % for managed sessions on 2s tick
2. When gentle/standard/aggressive threshold crossed, prune triggered automatically
3. Per-session threshold tracking prevents repeated triggers
4. Threshold state resets when usage drops below gentle threshold
5. Thresholds configurable via config.Load() (default 0.40/0.60/0.80)
6. Profile applied at launch (settings.local.json updated)
7. Profile cleaned up (ResetProfile) on clean session termination
8. ht doctor detects stale profiles from unclean termination
9. CLI commands that mutate sessions (prune, profile switch) route through daemon API
10. go test ./... passes

## File Context
Modify: internal/daemon/daemon.go (aggregator loop — threshold check goroutine)
Modify: internal/cli/launch.go (profile application)
Modify: internal/daemon/session_manager.go (profile cleanup on termination)

## Dependencies
Depends on Wave 12.4 (prune trigger, profile switch trigger).

## Testing Requirement
Test threshold monitor with mock context_usage data. Test auto-prune triggers at correct thresholds. Test profile cleanup on termination.
notes: Auto-prune disabled by setting thresholds to 1.0. Profile cleanup calls profiles.ResetProfile on clean exit.

## Why
Unit tests verify individual functions; integration tests verify the system works as a whole. SPEC-SYNC-INIT-DOCTOR section 10 specifies integration tests that must exist. Without these, the wiring between config/state/sync/doctor could be broken without detection.

## Spec Files

1. TestSync_FailsWithoutInit: calls runSyncInternal with missing project dir, verifies error contains "run: ht init"
2. TestSync_DryRun_ShowsPrerequisiteErrors: verifies dry-run surfaces missing prerequisite errors
3. TestDoctor_GreenOnCleanInstall: runs ht doctor against correctly initialized temp env, verifies exit 0
4. TestDoctor_FailsOnMissingSystemHooks: deletes system hook sync_entries row, verifies FAIL
5. TestDoctor_Fix_SystemHooks: same with --fix, verifies check repaired and row restored
6. Config resolution chain verified: env var overrides config file, flag overrides env var
7. Three-scope sync test: global rules → global settings, project rules → project settings, local rules → local settings
8. All grep assertions from SPEC-CONFIG-ARCHITECTURE section 7 pass

## Testing Requirement

- For doctor tests: set up various broken states (missing dir, missing hooks, stale profile) and verify detection
- For config tests: set env vars via t.Setenv(), write config.json to temp dirs, verify Load() output
- grep assertions can be run as exec.Command("grep", ...) in tests or verified by reading files

title: Wave 7.7: Duplicate event fix in executeLog
description:
## What
Remove the redundant EventLog call from executeLog in internal/engine/actions.go. Currently every log action produces two identical event rows — one from executeLog itself and one from the executor's logEvent call at the end of Execute.

## Why
D-004 / ISSUE-008: duplicate events pollute the events table, making ht events output confusing and event counts inaccurate. The executor's logEvent is the single source of truth — action functions should not write their own EventLog entries.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-SYNC-INIT-DOCTOR.md section 7 (complete before/after code)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. executeLog returns ActionResult{ActionTaken: "logged"} without calling actx.DB.EventLog()
2. After firing a log rule's hook event, exactly one row appears in the events table per execution
3. No other action functions (notify, deny, run) are changed
4. Existing tests in internal/engine/executor_test.go pass unchanged
5. go test ./internal/engine/... -count=1 passes

## File Context
Modify: internal/engine/actions.go (remove DB.EventLog call from executeLog — single-line removal)
Reference: internal/engine/executor.go (logEvent at end of Execute — remains unchanged)

## Dependencies
None — this is a standalone fix.

## Testing Requirement
Add TestExecuteLog_NoDoubleWrite per SPEC-SYNC-INIT-DOCTOR section 10: execute a rule with a log action via full Executor.Execute path against in-memory DB, count events, assert exactly 1 row.
notes:
Implementation hints:
- This is literally removing the if actx.DB != nil { actx.DB.EventLog(...) } block from executeLog
- The ActionResult{ActionTaken: "logged"} return stays — the executor reads this to populate its own logEvent call
- Verify no other action function writes EventLog (they don't, per spec, but confirm)
- The test should use state.OpenInMemory() and a real Executor to verify end-to-end

title: Wave 12.3: SSE hub upgrades + HTTP API surface
description:
## What

## Why
The SSE hub is the event distribution system — all four sources flow through it. The HTTP API is how all clients (CLI, dashboard, web UI) interact with the daemon.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. DaemonEvent has Source "io" for managed session events
2. GET /api/events streams all daemon events (cross-session)
3. SSE buffer raised to 5000
4. Slow consumers don't block daemon (drop-on-full)
5. All REST endpoints implemented: session list/show/spawn, message, interrupt, prune, profile, inject, flag, end, kill, attach, detach
6. 8-char prefix session lookup works in API
7. go test ./... passes

## File Context
Modify: internal/daemon/sse.go (buffer, global stream, Source field)
Modify or create: internal/daemon/api.go (full REST endpoint handlers)

## Dependencies

## Testing Requirement

1. ht doctor runs without error on a fresh ht init and prints all green checks
2. ht doctor --fix repairs missing system hooks and unsynced rules
3. ht events shows recent hook events; --rule, --since, --json flags work
4. ht rule add 'on pre-bash -> log' defaults to local scope (stores as local:<cwd>)
5. ht rule add --scope global '...' stores as global scope
6. Setting HT_PROXY_PORT=19800 causes ht proxy start to bind on port 19800
7. ~/.hitch/config.json with "proxy_port": 19800 overrides the default (when env var not set)
8. On a system with legacy state.db, first run renames it to hitch.db transparently
9. ht init twice does not produce duplicate system hooks in settings.json
10. ht sync with no prior ht init prints clear error message
11. grep -r '9800\|9801' internal/ returns no results outside internal/config/ and tests
12. go test ./... -count=1 passes, go vet ./... clean

## Testing Requirement
This IS the manual testing — run each acceptance criterion above as a hands-on test. Report pass/fail per criterion.
notes:
## Test Procedure
1. Start fresh: rm -rf ~/.hitch/ ~/.claude/settings.json (or use a temp HOME)
2. Run ht init — verify ~/.hitch/hitch.db created, system hooks in ~/.claude/settings.json
3. Run ht init again — verify no duplicate hooks (diff settings.json before/after)
4. Run ht doctor — all green
5. Add a rule: ht rule add 'on pre-bash -> log' — verify local scope
6. Run ht sync — verify rule installed in .claude/settings.local.json
7. Run ht events — verify the event log works
8. Test config override: export HT_PROXY_PORT=19800 && ht proxy start — verify port
9. Test config.json: write {"proxy_port": 19800} to ~/.hitch/config.json — verify
10. Test DB migration: rename hitch.db to state.db, run any ht command — verify auto-rename
11. Test grep assertions from SPEC-CONFIG-ARCHITECTURE section 7

title: Wave 12: Daemon Rewrite (SPEC-07-DAEMON-REVISED)
description:
Phase C orchestration layer — the capstone. Full 5-layer daemon rewrite per SPEC-07-DAEMON-REVISED.

## What
Session manager (subprocess I/O, NDJSON parsing), JSONL file watcher, session lifecycle state machine, full HTTP API surface, ht launch via daemon API, ht attach bidirectional I/O, all interaction triggers (prompt, interrupt, prune, profile switch, inject context, flag, end, kill), auto-pruning at context thresholds, systemd service install, six-panel operations console.

## Why
The daemon is the central nervous system that transforms Hitch from a collection of CLI tools into a control plane. It is the canonical agent process manager — web UIs, orchestrators, and messaging bridges build on top of its API.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-07-DAEMON-REVISED.md (primary — all sections)
- private/rev-3-daemon-brief.md (architectural sketch, north star use cases)
- private/rev-3-vision.md (Layer 5 daemon section)
- private/rev-3-decisions.md: D-009, D-011, D-036 through D-042

## Acceptance Criteria
1. SessionManager spawns claude subprocess with --input-format stream-json --output-format stream-json --verbose
2. Stdout reader uses 1MB+ buffer (scanner.Buffer call present)
3. Subscribe-before-send pattern enforced in SendPrompt and all interaction triggers
4. Session lifecycle: spawning→active→idle→attached→terminated transitions work
5. JSONL watcher discovers user-launched sessions and emits events
6. All 4 data sources active simultaneously (I/O stream, JSONL watcher, hook events, proxy rows)
7. ht launch calls daemon API (syscall.Exec path removed)
8. ht attach streams session output and forwards terminal input bidirectionally
9. POST /api/sessions/{id}/interrupt cancels current turn cleanly
10. POST /api/sessions/{id}/profile performs stop/apply/resume with conversation intact
11. Auto-pruning triggers at configured context thresholds
12. ht daemon install creates valid systemd user unit
13. SSE hub buffer raised to 5000
14. go test ./... -count=1 passes, go vet ./... clean

## File Context
New files: internal/daemon/session_manager.go, internal/daemon/watcher.go, internal/cli/attach.go
Major modifications: internal/daemon/daemon.go, internal/daemon/api.go, internal/cli/launch.go, internal/cli/watch.go, internal/cli/daemon.go
Existing to extend: internal/daemon/sse.go (hub changes), internal/daemon/tracker.go

## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests for SessionManager state transitions
- Unit tests for JSONL watcher file offset tracking
- Unit tests for subscribe-before-send ordering
- Unit tests for interaction trigger validation (wrong state → error)
- Integration tests: spawn session, send prompt, receive events, interrupt, terminate
- Integration test: daemon restart reconnects active sessions via --resume
notes:
Implementation order (15 steps):
1. Session state machine types and SQLite schema updates
2. internal/daemon/watcher.go — JSONL file watcher (Source 2)
3. internal/daemon/session_manager.go — subprocess spawning, goroutines, NDJSON parsing
4. Session lifecycle transitions (spawning→active→idle→attached→terminated)
5. SSE hub upgrades (Source io, global event stream, 5000 buffer)
6. HTTP API surface (REST endpoints for all interaction triggers)
7. ht launch rework (daemon API instead of syscall.Exec)
8. ht attach — bidirectional I/O command
9. Interaction triggers: send prompt, interrupt, prune, profile switch, inject context, flag, end, kill
10. Automatic pruning threshold monitor
11. Profile application at launch + cleanup at termination
12. CLI routing through daemon (D-011)
13. ht daemon install — systemd unit generation
14. Six-panel operations console (dashboard rewrite)
15. ht watch enhancements (source filtering, richer display)

Key constraints:
- Process group: cmd.SysProcAttr = &syscall.SysProcAttr{Setsid: true} so agent survives daemon crash
- Strip CLAUDECODE env var from subprocess (prevents nested session error)
- Set CLAUDE_CODE_ENTRYPOINT=hitch
- Idle timeout configurable via flat config key daemon_idle_timeout_minutes (default 5)
- Daemon API binds localhost only for v1 (D-041)

title: Wave 8.4: extends inheritance with cycle detection
description:
## What
Implement profile extends inheritance: resolve(), loadRaw(), mergeProfiles(), mergeHooks() in pkg/profiles/loader.go. Parent-first child-wins merge semantics for settings, env, envDeletes, hooks. Cycle detection via visited map. Update LoadAll to resolve inheritance.

## Why
ISSUE-016 / D-016: the Extends field is parsed and stored but never resolved. Profiles with extends silently ignore the parent, defeating the composability design.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED.md section 6 (complete code for resolve, loadRaw, mergeProfiles, mergeHooks)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED.md section 6.2 (merge semantics — 5 rules)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED.md section 6.4 (acceptance criteria)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. ht profile show my-safety (extends conservative) shows merged profile with conservative's env/settings plus child's hooks
2. ht profile switch my-safety installs both conservative's safety hook and child's Write guard hook
3. Multi-level chain resolves correctly (A extends B extends C)
4. Profile extending itself returns error, not hang or panic
5. Profile extending non-existent parent returns error from Load
6. Profile with no extends behaves identically to before
7. LoadAll resolves inheritance for each profile
8. Merged result has child's Name/Description/Tags, cleared Extends
9. go test ./... passes

## File Context
Modify: pkg/profiles/loader.go (split Load into loadRaw+resolve, add mergeProfiles, mergeHooks)

## Dependencies
Depends on Wave 8.2 (hooks wiring — mergeHooks produces hook entries that ApplyProfile must install).

## Testing Requirement
Per section 11: test extends resolution (single-level, multi-level chain, cycle detection, missing parent). Test mergeProfiles for all conflict cases (settings child-wins, env child-wins, hooks additive, delete-wins-over-parent).
notes:
Implementation hints:
- Load calls loadRaw then resolve(p, map[string]bool{name: true})
- resolve: if no extends return p; check visited for cycle; loadRaw parent; resolve parent recursively; mergeProfiles(parent, child)
- mergeProfiles: settings parent base + child overrides (nil wins), env parent base + child overrides, envDeletes union, hooks additive via mergeHooks
- mergeHooks: for each event, child entries appended after parent entries (concat slices)
- LoadAll must call Load (which resolves) instead of direct unmarshal
- After config migration, use cfg.ProfilesDir instead of hardcoded ~/.hitch/profiles/

title: Wave 10.2: Strategy interface + registry + pipeline
description:
## What
Implement Strategy interface, StrategyInfo, registry map, register() function, and prescriptions (tier→strategy order) in registry.go. Implement the pipeline in pipeline.go: runPrescription(), executeActions(), T1.5 guard (byte savings threshold), T1.4 relinking (orphaned tool results), fixOrphanedToolResults().

## Why
The pipeline is the execution engine that runs strategies in order, applies their actions safely, and ensures structural integrity after modifications. The three mandatory safety mechanisms (isProtected, T1.5 guard, T1.4 relinking) prevent corruption.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE.md section 4 (Strategy interface, registry, prescriptions with ordering rationale)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE.md section 6 (pipeline — runPrescription, executeActions, fixOrphanedToolResults)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE.md section 5.2 (T1.5 guard — minimum byte savings)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE.md section 5.3 (T1.4 relinking — orphaned tool results)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Strategy type is func([]Message, PruneConfig) StrategyResult
2. register() adds strategies to the global registry map
3. Prescriptions define gentle/standard/aggressive tiers with correct ordering
4. runPrescription runs strategies in order, passing output of each to the next
5. executeActions applies PruneActions to message list (remove or replace), respecting isProtected
6. T1.5 guard: skip action if bytes saved < threshold (configurable)
7. T1.4 relinking: after removing a tool_use message, find its orphaned tool_result and remove it too
8. fixOrphanedToolResults runs after all strategies, cleaning up any remaining orphans
9. go test ./internal/pruning/... passes

## File Context
Create: internal/pruning/registry.go, internal/pruning/pipeline.go
Create: internal/pruning/pipeline_test.go

## Dependencies
Depends on Wave 10.1 (types, message I/O, helpers).

## Testing Requirement
Unit tests: pipeline with mock strategies producing known actions. Test isProtected messages are never modified. Test T1.4 relinking removes orphaned tool_results. Test T1.5 guard skips small savings.
notes:
Implementation hints:
- executeActions: sort actions by LineIndex descending to avoid index shifting during removal
- T1.4: when removing a tool_use msg, search forward for tool_result with matching tool_use_id
- T1.5: configurable minimum bytes — skip action if OriginalBytes - PrunedBytes < threshold
- Phase 1: all 5 strategies in gentle prescription. Standard/aggressive are gentle + future Phase 2 strategies.
- fixOrphanedToolResults: after all strategies, scan for tool_result with no matching tool_use

title: Wave 7.10: Wire config.Load() across all CLI commands
description:
## What
Replace the existing resolvePaths() function in internal/cli/config.go with config.Load() calls. Update the Paths struct to derive from Config. Wire config.Load(flagOverrides) at the top of every Cobra command handler that needs configuration. Ensure cfg is passed down — never stored as a package-level global.

## Why
SPEC-CONFIG-ARCHITECTURE section 4.4: the config package is useless if callers still use resolvePaths(). This task completes the migration by making config.Load() the single entry point for all configuration access. It's the "last mile" that connects the config package to the rest of the CLI.

1. resolvePaths() is either removed or reduced to a thin wrapper around config.Load()
2. Every CLI command that opens DB or accesses paths calls config.Load(flagOverrides) first

4. Setting HT_DB_PATH=/tmp/hitch-test/test.db causes ht commands to use that database
5. ~/.hitch/config.json with "proxy_port": 19800 produces same behavior as env var (when env not set)

title: Tryout: Wave 10 pruning engine
description:
## What
Manual verification of Wave 10 deliverables: ht prune on a real session, backup creation, all 5 Phase 1 strategies, safety mechanisms (isProtected, T1.5 guard, T1.4 relinking), atomic write, file locking, prune event logging.

## Why
Human review gate — validates the pruning engine before Wave 12 (daemon rewrite) integrates auto-pruning at context thresholds. Pruning is destructive (modifies JSONL in place) so correctness is critical.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE.md (all sections — especially sections 5-7 for safety and strategies, section 12 for CLI)
- private/rev-3-decisions.md: D-012, D-031 through D-035

## Acceptance Criteria (Manual Verification)
1. ht prune <session-id> --tier gentle runs and produces savings summary showing all 5 strategies
2. Backup file created at <session>.YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS.jsonl.bak (verify timestamped backup exists)
3. Only 3 most recent backups kept (run prune 4+ times, verify old backups deleted)
4. Protected messages preserved: isCompactSummary, compact_boundary, content-replacement, marble-origami messages all survive pruning
5. T1.5 guard: after pruning, no tool_result block references a tool_use_id that doesn't exist in any surviving message
6. T1.4 relinking: after pruning, no parentUuid points to a removed message's uuid
7. compact-summary-collapse: pre-boundary messages removed, post-boundary and singleton metadata preserved
8. tool-use-result-strip: toolUseResult top-level field stripped from messages
9. Atomic write verified: session file is valid JSONL after pruning (parse every line)
10. Prune events logged to SQLite (query events table for type='prune') and disk (check ~/.hitch/logs/prune/)
11. go test ./... -count=1 passes, go vet ./... clean

## File Context
Key files to inspect: internal/pruning/ (all files), internal/cli/prune.go, internal/pruning/strategies/*.go, internal/pruning/testdata/

## Testing Requirement
This IS the manual testing — run ht prune on real session files. Inspect the JSONL before and after to verify safety mechanisms. Use jq to validate structural integrity.
notes:
## Test Procedure
1. Pick a substantive session with compaction boundary (find one with isCompactSummary)
2. cp the JSONL file to a backup location for comparison
3. Run: ht prune <id> --tier gentle — record savings summary
4. Diff original vs pruned: verify protected messages preserved
5. jq validation: cat pruned.jsonl | while read line; do echo "$line" | jq . > /dev/null || echo "INVALID: $line"; done
6. Verify parentUuid integrity: extract all uuids and parentUuids, check no parentUuid points to missing uuid
7. Verify tool_use/tool_result pairing: extract all tool_use_ids from tool_result blocks, verify each has a matching tool_use block
8. Run prune 4 times to verify backup rotation (only 3 newest kept)
9. Check prune logs: ls ~/.hitch/logs/prune/ and sqlite3 ~/.hitch/hitch.db "SELECT * FROM prune_events ORDER BY timestamp DESC LIMIT 5"
10. Test all tiers: --tier gentle, --tier standard, --tier aggressive
11. Test --json output mode

## Key Spec References
- SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE section 5: safety mechanisms (isProtected, T1.5, T1.4)
- SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE section 7.1-7.5: individual strategy algorithms
- SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE section 9: JSONL I/O and file safety (atomic write, backups)
- SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE section 12: CLI specification
- SPEC-PRUNING-ENGINE section 15: logging (SQLite + disk)

description:
## What
Manual verification of Wave 11 deliverables in two phases: 11A (proxy CLI restructuring: ht transactions tree, ht proxy errors rename, command migrations, shared helpers) and 11B (P2 intelligence: billing blocks, extraction pipeline, AI summarization, full report suite, session index).

## Acceptance Criteria (Manual Verification)
### 11A — Proxy CLI
1. ht transactions list|show|inspect are valid commands with --json support
2. ht proxy errors replaces ht proxy gaps (ht proxy gaps returns unknown command)
3. ht proxy errors has no 60-second silence scan (verify help text, verify no scan in code)

### 11B — Intelligence P2
11. ht costs blocks shows 5-hour billing windows with burn rate and projection
12. ht sessions extract produces JSON files at ~/.hitch/sessions/

title: Wave 8.3: Settings delete restoration (ISSUE-015)
description:
## What
Fix ResetProfile to handle settings_delete: tracked key prefix. When ApplyProfile encounters a nil value in p.Settings (JSON null = delete), save the original value before deleting. On reset, restore the original. Add pkg/settings functions: GetKey, SetKey, DeleteKey, SetHookEntries, DeleteHookEntries.

## Why
ISSUE-015: if a profile deletes a settings key (sets to null), switching away leaves the key permanently deleted. The reset path handles env: and settings: prefixes but skips settings_delete: entries entirely.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED.md section 5 (complete fix code for ApplyProfile settings loop and ResetProfile with all prefix handlers)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED.md section 10 (pkg/settings functions: GetKey, SetKey, SetHookEntries, DeleteHookEntries)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. A profile with "settings": {"someKey": null} deletes someKey on apply
2. After ht profile switch --reset, someKey is restored to its pre-profile value
3. If someKey was absent before the profile, it remains absent after reset
4. ResetProfile handles all five tracked key prefixes: env:, env_delete:, settings:, settings_delete:, hook:
5. pkg/settings has: GetKey, SetKey (new), DeleteKey (if not present), SetHookEntries, DeleteHookEntries
6. A profile with no settings deletes behaves identically to before this change
7. go test ./... passes

## File Context
Modify: pkg/profiles/apply.go (ApplyProfile settings loop — save originals for nil values; ResetProfile — handle settings_delete: and hook: prefixes)
Modify or create: pkg/settings/ (GetKey, SetKey, SetHookEntries, DeleteHookEntries)

## Dependencies
Depends on Wave 8.2 (hooks wiring and active_profiles table must exist for originals storage).

## Testing Requirement
Per section 11: test settings_delete originals are saved and restored. Test ResetProfile with settings_delete prefix restores original value. Test absent key stays absent.
notes:
Implementation hints:
- In ApplyProfile settings loop: check v == nil → save original via GetKey, store as settings_delete:KEY in originals map
- ResetProfile settings_delete handler: unmarshal original JSON, call SetKey to restore
- ResetProfile hook handler: unmarshal original hook entries, call SetHookEntries; if no original, call DeleteHookEntries
- After all restores, ResetProfile should re-run ht sync for this scope (see spec section 5.3 final note)

title: Wave 8.2: Hooks wiring in ApplyProfile + active_profiles SQLite table
description:
## What
Fix ApplyProfile to actually process p.Hooks (currently ignored — ISSUE-014). Add hooks merge into settings.local.json with tracked key prefix hook:<EventName>. Create active_profiles SQLite table (D-044) replacing the JSON file. Add *state.DB parameter to ApplyProfile, ResetProfile, CurrentProfile. Add required pkg/settings functions: GetHookEntries, AddHookEntries.

## Why
ISSUE-014: conservative profile declares a PreToolUse hook that is never installed. Users believe they have safety guardrails; they do not. This is the critical bug fix for profiles. D-044 mandates SQLite as single state store — the active-profile.json file must be replaced.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED.md section 4 (complete hooks wiring code in ApplyProfile)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED.md section 4.3 (active_profiles SQLite table schema, ActiveProfileRecord struct, function signatures)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED.md section 10 (new pkg/settings functions table)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. ht profile switch conservative installs PreToolUse Bash deny-hook in .claude/settings.local.json
2. ht profile switch default (after conservative) removes the conservative hook without leaving stale entries
3. Non-profile hooks in settings.local.json written by ht sync are preserved across profile switches
4. active_profiles table exists in SQLite with columns: project_dir (PK), profile_name, tracked_keys (JSON), originals (JSON), applied_at
5. ApplyProfile writes tracked keys and originals to SQLite, not JSON file
6. ResetProfile reads from SQLite, deletes row after reset
7. CurrentProfile queries SQLite by project_dir
8. File-based readActiveProfile/writeActiveProfile removed
9. go test ./... passes

## File Context
Modify: pkg/profiles/apply.go (hooks loop in ApplyProfile, SQLite calls replacing file I/O, export ActiveProfileRecord)
Modify: internal/state/db.go (active_profiles table in schema)
Create or modify: pkg/settings/ (GetHookEntries, AddHookEntries functions)
Modify: internal/cli/profile.go (remove duplicate activeProfileRecord type, pass db to Apply/Reset/Current)

## Dependencies
Depends on Wave 8.1 (scope fix — must know correct scope before wiring hooks).

## Testing Requirement
Per section 11: test ApplyProfile with profile containing hooks writes to hooks block; test ResetProfile removes profile hooks; test that non-profile hooks survive switch. Use state.OpenInMemory() and t.TempDir().
notes:
Implementation hints:
- Hook tracking key prefix: hook:<EventName> — one per event type the profile contributed
- GetHookEntries operates on s.raw["hooks"] (raw JSON map level), not typed Settings.Hooks field
- AddHookEntries appends to existing array, creates hooks object and event array if not present
- ActiveProfileRecord: export from pkg/profiles/apply.go, remove duplicate from internal/cli/profile.go
- Function sigs change: ApplyProfile(p, projectDir, db), ResetProfile(rec, projectDir, db), CurrentProfile(projectDir, db)

title: Wave 13: Settings TUI Rework (SPEC-SETTINGS-TUI-REWORK)
description:

## What
Viewport + window size propagation for all tabs, Escape navigation with exit confirmation, three-tier bubbling (modified→quick toggles→collapsed), Instructions tab (tab 6), output modes (--plain/--text/--json), Explorer collapsible hierarchy with lazy loading and file viewer.

## Why
Current TUI has no viewport clipping (depends on terminal scrollback), no bubbling (dumps all settings flat), missing hooks wiring display, and no way to see instruction sources. This makes it unusable for understanding configuration.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-SETTINGS-TUI-REWORK.md (primary — all 9 sections)
- private/rev-3-decisions.md: D-027, D-028, D-029, D-030

title: Wave 7.6: Migrate all hardcoded paths/ports to internal/config/
description:
## What
Replace all hardcoded path literals, port numbers, and inline path construction across the codebase with references to internal/config/ package values. This is the bulk migration step that touches ~20 locations across 8+ files.

## Why
SPEC-CONFIG-ARCHITECTURE section 4.1 audits every hardcoded value and mandates that no path or port string exists outside internal/config/defaults.go and test files. Without this, the config resolution chain is dead code — nothing actually reads from it.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-CONFIG-ARCHITECTURE.md section 4.1 (hardcoded values audit table — 20+ locations)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-CONFIG-ARCHITECTURE.md section 4.3 (proxy log directory consolidation)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-CONFIG-ARCHITECTURE.md section 4.4 (how to migrate a caller — pattern)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-CONFIG-ARCHITECTURE.md section 4.5 (migration ordering — keep build green)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. grep -r '"\.hitch"' internal/ returns no results (all path construction through config)
2. grep -r '9800\|9801' internal/ returns no results outside internal/config/defaults.go and test files
3. grep -r 'state\.db\|credentials\.enc\|manifest\.json\|proxy-logs' internal/ returns no hardcoded path strings outside internal/config/ and test fixtures
4. internal/proxy/server.go accepts log dir and pid path as constructor parameters (not inline)
5. internal/pricing/pricing.go accepts pricing path as parameter to LoadPricing()
6. internal/daemon/pid.go accepts pid path as parameter
7. Proxy log directory moved from ~/.hitch/proxy-logs to cfg.LogDir/proxy/ with migration
8. go build ./... succeeds, go vet ./... clean
9. go test ./... -count=1 passes — no regressions

## File Context
Modify: internal/cli/config.go, internal/cli/proxy.go (6 locations), internal/cli/launch.go (2 locations), internal/cli/daemon.go (2 locations), internal/cli/profile.go (3 locations), internal/proxy/server.go (2 locations), internal/pricing/pricing.go (1 location), internal/daemon/pid.go (1 location)
Reference: SPEC-CONFIG-ARCHITECTURE section 4.1 audit table for exact line numbers

## Dependencies
Depends on Wave 7.1 (internal/config/ package must exist first).

## Testing Requirement
Existing tests must continue to pass. Add tests for proxy log migration path if applicable. Verify grep acceptance criteria pass as automated check.
notes:
Implementation hints:
- Follow SPEC-CONFIG-ARCHITECTURE section 4.5 migration ordering to keep build green throughout
- Pattern per section 4.4: call config.Load(flagOverrides) at top of Cobra handler, pass cfg down, delete old inline construction
- For non-CLI packages (proxy, pricing, daemon): accept values as constructor params, don't call config.Load() inside the package
- Proxy log consolidation: move ~/.hitch/proxy-logs → cfg.LogDir/proxy/ with one-time migration on first startup
- config.go resolvePaths() replacement is the hub — fixing it first removes the most duplication
- ManifestPath removed per D-044 — grep for manifest references and clean up

title: Tryout: Wave 12 daemon rewrite
description:
## What
Manual verification of Wave 12 deliverables: daemon rewrite including SessionManager subprocess model, JSONL file watcher, session lifecycle state machine, full HTTP API surface, ht launch via daemon API, ht attach bidirectional I/O, interaction triggers, auto-pruning at thresholds, systemd install, six-panel operations console.

## Why
Human review gate — Wave 12 is the capstone that transforms Hitch from CLI tools into a control plane. The daemon must correctly manage agent subprocesses, stream events, and handle all interaction triggers before the web UI (Wave 14) can build on top.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-07-DAEMON-REVISED.md (all layers 1-5, all sections)

- private/rev-3-decisions.md: D-009, D-011, D-036 through D-042

## Acceptance Criteria (Manual Verification)
1. ht launch --profile performance "write hello world" spawns claude subprocess via daemon API (verify with ht proxy status showing daemon-managed session)
2. ht attach <session-id> streams session output and allows typing prompts bidirectionally
3. ht watch <8-char-prefix> shows events from all four sources (io, jsonl, hooks, proxy) with source tags
4. POST /api/sessions/{id}/interrupt cancels current turn cleanly (session continues)
5. POST /api/sessions/{id}/profile performs stop/apply/resume with conversation intact (verify context preserved)
6. Auto-pruning triggers at configured gentle threshold (set to 0.05 for testing; verify prune event logged)
7. User-launched sessions (not via ht launch) are discovered by JSONL watcher and appear in event stream
8. ht daemon install creates valid systemd user unit (systemctl --user cat hitch-daemon shows unit)
9. SSE hub buffer at 5000 (verify in code: grep for buffer size)
10. Stdout reader uses 1MB+ buffer (verify: grep scanner.Buffer in session_manager.go)
11. Dashboard (ht dashboard or keybinding) shows six-panel operations console
12. go test ./... -count=1 passes, go vet ./... clean

## File Context
Key files: internal/daemon/session_manager.go, internal/daemon/watcher.go, internal/daemon/api.go, internal/daemon/daemon.go, internal/daemon/sse.go, internal/cli/launch.go, internal/cli/attach.go, internal/cli/watch.go, internal/cli/daemon.go

## Testing Requirement
This IS the manual testing — requires running the daemon, launching sessions, attaching, and testing interaction triggers in real time. Most criteria cannot be verified by automated tests alone.
notes:
## Test Procedure
1. Start daemon: ht daemon start (or systemctl --user start hitch-daemon)
2. Launch a session: ht launch --profile performance "list files in current directory"
3. Verify session appears: ht sessions list (should show managed session)
4. Attach: ht attach <id> — type a follow-up prompt, verify bidirectional I/O
5. Detach with Ctrl-D, then ht watch <id> — verify event stream continues
6. Test interrupt: start a long task, then POST /api/sessions/{id}/interrupt (curl or ht cli)
7. Test profile switch: POST /api/sessions/{id}/profile {"profile": "minimal"} — verify session resumes
8. Test auto-pruning: set gentle threshold to 0.05 in config.json, run a session past that context level
9. Launch claude manually (not via ht launch) — verify JSONL watcher discovers it
10. ht daemon install — verify systemd unit created
11. ht dashboard — verify six panels render

## Key Spec References
- SPEC-07-DAEMON-REVISED Layer 1: four data sources (sections 1.1-1.5)
- SPEC-07-DAEMON-REVISED Layer 2: SessionManager (sections 2.1-2.8)

title: Wave 8.1: Profile scope inversion fix (--project → --global)
description:
## What
Fix the scope inversion bug in internal/cli/profile.go: rename --project flag to --global, invert the boolean logic in profileProjectDir so local scope (CWD) is the default instead of global scope (home dir). Update all flag reads and scope display strings.

## Why
ISSUE-011/012/013: profileProjectDir returns os.UserHomeDir() when useProject=false (the default), making global scope the default. The intended design is local scope as default. The flag name --project implies local is opt-in when it should be the default.

## Spec Files
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED.md section 3 (complete fix with code, flag renames, scope display strings)
- private/rev-3-specs/SPEC-06-PROFILES-REVISED.md section 3.3 (DSL switch_profile consistency — no code change needed after this fix)

## Acceptance Criteria
1. ht profile switch performance (no flags) writes to .claude/settings.local.json in CWD
2. ht profile switch performance --global writes to ~/.claude/settings.local.json
3. ht profile current (no flags) shows the local-scoped active profile
4. ht profile list (no flags) marks the local-scoped active profile
5. DSL switch_profile and CLI ht profile switch target the same file (local scope)
6. No references to --project flag remain in profile commands
7. go test ./... passes

## File Context
Modify: internal/cli/profile.go (profileProjectDir, newProfileListCmd, newProfileSwitchCmd, newProfileCurrentCmd, runProfileSwitch scope display strings)

## Dependencies

## Testing Requirement

notes:

- profileProjectDir(useGlobal bool): if useGlobal return os.UserHomeDir(), else return os.Getwd()
- Three flag sites to rename: newProfileListCmd, newProfileSwitchCmd, newProfileCurrentCmd
- All cmd.Flags().GetBool("project") → cmd.Flags().GetBool("global")
- Scope display string: default is now .claude/settings.local.json, --global is ~/.claude/settings.local.json
